questions.question_text: In Montana, which licensees must carry errors and omissions insurance?
distractors: Only brokers
Only licensees handling trust funds
Only salespersons with five or more years
questions.explanation: Under MCA 37-51-325, all active Montana real estate licensees are required to carry errors and omissions insurance. There are no exemptions based on license type or experience level.

questions.question_text: What is the minimum per-claim coverage under Montana's group E&O insurance plan?
questions.explanation: Montana's group errors and omissions insurance plan provides minimum coverage of $100,000 per claim and $300,000 in aggregate. Independent policies must meet or exceed these minimums.

questions.question_text: A Montana licensee obtains independent E&O coverage with a $75,000 per-claim limit. Does this meet state requirements?
distractors: Yes, any E&O policy satisfies it
Yes, if aggregate exceeds $300,000
No, independent policies are not allowed
questions.explanation: Independent E&O coverage must meet or exceed the group plan minimums of $100,000 per claim and $300,000 aggregate. A $75,000 per-claim limit falls short of the minimum requirement.

questions.question_text: A Montana licensee wants independent E&O coverage instead of the group plan. What condition must the policy meet?
questions.correct_answer: It must meet or exceed group plan minimums
distractors: It must be from a Montana-based insurer
It must cover intentional fraud claims
It must include coverage for unlicensed activity
questions.explanation: Independent E&O coverage must meet or exceed the group plan minimums of $100,000 per claim and $300,000 aggregate. Licensees can choose independent coverage as long as it provides at least this level of protection.

questions.question_text: A licensee intentionally misrepresents a property's condition and a buyer sues. Will the E&O policy cover this claim?
distractors: Yes, all claims against licensees qualify
Yes, if the claim involves condition
Only if the licensee files a counterclaim
questions.explanation: Montana E&O insurance covers negligent acts, errors, and omissions but explicitly excludes intentional fraud and willful misconduct. The licensee would be personally liable for intentional misrepresentation.

questions.question_text: When must a Montana licensee provide proof of E&O coverage to the Board?
distractors: Only when a claim is filed
Within 90 days of initial licensure
Every five years during an audit cycle
questions.explanation: Montana licensees must provide proof of current E&O insurance coverage at the time of license renewal. The Board may also verify coverage at any time during the licensing period.

questions.question_text: A licensee's E&O policy lapses on March 15 and she does not obtain new coverage. What happens to her license?
distractors: Nothing until the next renewal date
She receives a 60-day grace period
Her license is permanently revoked
questions.explanation: A lapse in E&O coverage results in automatic inactivation of the Montana real estate license. The licensee cannot practice until valid coverage is reinstated and proof is provided to the Board.

questions.question_text: Can the Montana Board of Realty Regulation verify a licensee's E&O coverage outside the renewal period?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the Board can verify anytime
distractors: No, only during the renewal window
Only after a formal complaint is filed
Only during scheduled biennial audits
questions.explanation: The Board of Realty Regulation has the authority to verify a licensee's E&O insurance status at any time, not just during renewal. Licensees must maintain continuous coverage throughout their active license period.

questions.question_text: Under Montana law, a licensee who knowingly allows E&O coverage to lapse has committed what?
distractors: A civil tort only
No offense if no claims arise
A federal insurance violation
questions.explanation: Allowing E&O insurance to lapse is a violation of Montana real estate license law under MCA 37-51-325. The Board may take disciplinary action in addition to the automatic license inactivation.

questions.question_text: A Montana broker's E&O policy lapsed last month. She wants to resume practicing. What must she do first?
questions.correct_answer: Obtain valid coverage and notify Board
distractors: Wait until the next renewal cycle
Pay a reinstatement fine of $500
Complete eight hours of continuing education
questions.explanation: To reactivate a license inactivated due to E&O lapse, the licensee must obtain valid E&O coverage meeting minimum requirements and provide proof to the Board before resuming any real estate activity.
